title: Hair Oiling Done Right: How, When, and Which Oil
excerpt: Hair oiling is one of Ayurveda's oldest daily rituals, and a few small details, when done right and how often, make all the difference between nourishment and buildup.
body_html:
a practice most of us grew up watching

For many of us, the image is familiar: a grandmother warming oil in a small bowl, sitting someone down and working it through the scalp with slow, deliberate fingers. It felt like love, and in Ayurveda it is also medicine. The practice is called shiro-abhyanga, meaning 'anointing the head', and the use of medicated or plain oils on the scalp and hair is described under the broader category of murdha taila, the application of oil to the head.

The Ashtanga Hridaya, one of the foundational Ayurvedic texts, lists murdha taila among the daily health-preserving routines (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). It says that regular oil application to the head prevents headaches, premature greying, hair loss and dryness of the scalp, and keeps the sense organs sharp. The Caraka Samhita similarly recommends daily oil application as part of a dinacharya, or daily regimen, noting that it strengthens the roots of the hair and keeps the scalp free from disease (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 5).

A consistent twice-a-week habit over months does more than an occasional long overnight soak.

what the classical texts actually recommend

Ayurveda does not prescribe a single universal oil. The choice depends on your prakriti (constitution) and your current imbalance. The Sushruta Samhita and the Ashtanga Hridaya both describe oils suited to different conditions and body types (Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsasthana; Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

Sesame oil (tila taila) is described as the base of choice for most Vata-type concerns: dry scalp, brittle hair, flaking, and hair that breaks easily. It is warming, heavy, and penetrating. For Pitta-related issues, which show up as an oily or inflamed scalp, premature greying, or heat-related hair fall, coconut oil is cooling and tends to work better. Brahmi oil and bhringraj oil, both prepared with a sesame or coconut base and specific herbs, appear in classical formulations for promoting hair growth and calming an overheated scalp. For Kapha types, where the scalp tends to be oily and the hair dense but prone to product buildup, lighter oils and less frequent application make more sense.

The Bhavaprakasha Nighantu documents the properties of many of these herbs used in hair oils, including bhringraj (Eclipta alba), which is consistently listed as a rasayana for hair, and brahmi (Bacopa monnieri), which is described as beneficial for both the scalp and the mind (Bhavaprakasha, Haritakyadi Varga).

how and when to oil: the practical part

The classical texts recommend oiling the hair in the morning before bathing, at least a couple of hours before you wash it off. The Ashtanga Hridaya suggests this as part of a pre-bath morning routine (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Warm the oil slightly: it penetrates better and feels far more soothing than cold oil straight from a bottle.

Apply it at the scalp first, not the lengths. Use your fingertips to work it in with gentle circular motion across the crown, the temples, and the nape. This is the shiro-abhyanga technique: the massage itself stimulates local circulation and is considered as important as the oil. Once the scalp is covered, you can run whatever remains through the lengths of your hair. Leave it on for at least thirty minutes. If you can manage a couple of hours, better. Overnight oiling is mentioned in the classical literature but may not suit everyone, particularly those with a Kapha tendency or a damp climate.

On frequency: daily oiling is the classical ideal and the Caraka Samhita mentions it without qualification (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 5). In practice, most modern schedules allow two to three times a week, and this is entirely workable. The key is regularity over intensity. A consistent twice-a-week habit over months does more than an occasional long overnight soak.

A few things to avoid: do not oil immediately after a hot shower when the scalp is already producing sebum and the pores are open in a different way. Do not leave oil on for multiple days without washing, as this can clog follicles and cause scalp irritation. And if you are using a herbal oil, check that it has been properly prepared, not just scented with fragrance, since raw herb infusions in a carrier oil behave very differently from a commercial product that adds plant extracts to mineral oil.

when to see a doctor or qualified vaidya

Hair oiling is a nourishing practice, but it is not a treatment for medical conditions. If you are experiencing sudden or rapid hair loss, patchy bald spots, significant scalp inflammation, persistent itching, thick scaly patches, or hair fall that has worsened over weeks despite lifestyle care, please consult a dermatologist or a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner. These signs can indicate conditions such as alopecia areata, scalp infections, hormonal imbalances, nutritional deficiencies, or thyroid disorders that need proper diagnosis and management.

This article shares general information from the classical Ayurvedic tradition. It is not a diagnosis and not a substitute for medical care. If you are pregnant, have a skin condition, or are on medication, check with your doctor before using any herbal oil formulation.

Try This Today

  • Warm your oil first: Pour a small amount into a steel or glass bowl and set it in a cup of hot water for two minutes. Warm oil absorbs into the scalp more easily and the experience is far more grounding.
  • Start at the crown, work outward: Dip your fingertips into the warm oil and press gently into the scalp at the top of the head. Move in slow circles toward the temples, then the nape of the neck. Five minutes of actual massage is more effective than ten minutes of just coating the hair.
  • Match oil to your scalp, not your hair type: If your scalp is dry and flaky, sesame oil. If it tends to be itchy and warm, coconut oil. If it is oily, use a light oil sparingly and focus massage on the scalp only, keeping lengths mostly dry.
  • Try a bhringraj or brahmi oil for sustained care: Look for oils where these herbs are properly infused into a sesame or coconut base, not just listed as fragrance ingredients. One reliable check: the oil should have a slight herbal smell and a slightly darker color than plain carrier oil.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Hair oiling is one of Ayurveda's oldest daily rituals, and a few small details, when done right and how often, make all the difference between nourishment and buildup.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Warm sesame oil suits dry, Vata hair; coconut oil suits an inflamed or heat-prone scalp.
Apply at the scalp first with gentle circular massage, leave for at least 30 minutes before washing.
Consistency matters more than duration: twice a week, every week, is better than one long overnight soak.

glossary_terms.definition: Therapeutic head massage with oil; shiro means head, abhyanga means oil application with rubbing.
The classical category of oil treatments applied to the head, described in several Ayurvedic texts as part of daily and therapeutic routines.
The Ayurvedic concept of a daily health regimen, covering morning routines from waking to eating.
An individual's constitutional type, determined by the balance of the three doshas at birth.
A class of Ayurvedic preparations or practices considered rejuvenating and longevity-promoting.
Sesame oil; tila is the Sanskrit name for the sesame plant, and taila means oil.

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Shiro-abhyanga" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Shiro-abhyanga is defined as: Therapeutic head massage with oil; shiro means head, abhyanga means oil application with rubbing.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to warm your oil first. Pour a small amount into a steel or glass bowl and set it in a cup of hot water for two minutes. Warm oil absorbs into the scalp more easily and the experience is far more grounding.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to start at the crown, work outward. Dip your fingertips into the warm oil and press gently into the scalp at the top of the head. Move in slow circles toward the temples, then the nape of the neck. Five minutes of actual massage is more effective than ten minutes of just coating the hair.
